## Hyperplanes 

## Planes in 3D

In $\mathbb{R}^3$, a plane is a collection of points lying on a flat surface. 
We can obtain a plane $\Pi$ as follows 

$$
\Pi = \{ x \mid x = p_0 + \alpha u + \beta v,\; \alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R} \}
$$

where $u, v \in \mathbb{R}^3$ are linearly independent vectors determining how $\Pi$ is oriented, and $p_0$ is a pass through point.

::: {#fig-plane-widget}
{{< include _shared/plane_widget.qmd >}}
Interactive 3D plane: rotate the direction vectors, move \(p_0\), and inspect the normal.
:::

Depending on the values of $u$, $v$ and $p_0$, then $\Pi$ is either a subspace or an affine subspace.

- If $p_0 \in \text{span}\{u, v\}$, then $\exists \gamma, \omega \in \mathbb{R} \mid p_0 = \gamma u + \omega v$. 
We would then be able to write
$$
\begin{align}
x &= p_0 + \alpha u + \beta v \\
& = \gamma u + \omega v + \alpha u + \beta v \\ 
&= (\gamma + \alpha) u + (\omega + \beta) v \\ 
&= \alpha^{\prime} u + \beta^{\prime} v  
\end{align}
$$
and therefore $\Pi = \text{span}\{u, v\}$. As a consquence, $\Pi$ forms a subspace in these cases.

- On the contrary, when $p_0 \notin \text{span}\{u, v\}$, then $\Pi$ $ does not pass through the origin.
In this case, $\Pi$ is rather an affine subspace.

For any plane, there exists a normal vector $N$ such that for any two points $p_A, p_B \in \Pi$:

$$N \cdot (p_A - p_B) = 0$$

This means that $N$ is orthogonal to every vector in $\Pi$, typically written as $N \perp \Pi$.
In $\mathbb{R}^3$, we can obtain the normal vector as $N = u \times v$, which gives a vector that is orthogonal to both $u$ and $v$.

We can further develop the previous formula

$$N \cdot p_A = N \cdot p_B$$

Since this holds $\forall p_A, p_B \in \Pi$, all points in the plane have the same value of $N \cdot p$. 
Therefore, there exists a constant $d$ such that

$$N \cdot p = d,\,\forall p \in \Pi$$

Therefore, this equation provides a characterization of the plane.
In other words, given any generic point $x \in \mathbb{R}^3$, if $N \cdot x = d$ then we know that $x \in \Pi$. 
We can then write

$$
\Pi = \{ x \in \mathbb{R}^3 \mid N \cdot x = d \}
$$

If $N = \begin{pmatrix} a \\ b \\ c \end{pmatrix}$ then condition $N \cdot x = d$ becomes:

$$
\Pi: ax + by + cz = d
$$

This is the Cartesian equation of the plane.
Since the equation holds for any point in the plane, we can define $d = N \cdot p_0$.
It turns out that $d$ is proportional to the distance from the plane to the origin.

In this system we have one equation and three unknowns, so the solution space is two-dimensional.
This is consistent with our initial description saying that the solution was either a subspace formed by $\text{span}\{u, v\}$, or affine transformation of it.
In particular, solving the homogenous system by isolating $z$ we can prove that $u = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ -\frac{a}{c}\end{pmatrix}$ and $v = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ -\frac{b}{c}\end{pmatrix}$.


## Hyperplanes

In $\mathbb{R}^n$, we define a hyperplane can be defined as:

$$
\Pi = \{ x \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid N \cdot x = d \}
$$

We now have $n$ unknowns, and for $N = \begin{pmatrix}a_1 \\ \vdots \\ a_n \end{pmatrix}$ it holds that:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^n a_i x_i = d
$$

In particular, if $p_0 \in \Pi$, then $d = N \cdot p_0$.

Since one linear constraint is imposed, the solution space, and therefore the hyperplane, have dimension $n - 1$.

As before, $N$ is orthogonal to every direction contained in the hyperplane.
 
$$\text{dist}(x, \Pi) = \frac{|N \cdot x + d|}{\|N\|}$$
